analysis.theory_role: Presents poststructuralism as a critical sociocultural theory that deconstructs traditional understandings of the subject, focusing on how identity is constructed through discourse, power, and subjectification.
analysis.source_role: Seminar slides designed to apply poststructuralist concepts (Foucault, discourse, power) to concrete cases like historical psychiatry and modern gender dynamics (the Manosphere).
analysis.relation_to_lecture: Acts as the seminar application for Week 10, bridging theoretical concepts of poststructuralism with active group work on Foucault's texts and a video case study.

central_claims.claim: Personality or identity is not an innate essence one 'is', but rather something one 'does' and is subjected to through mechanisms of subjectification and power.
Discourses establish the conditions for how we can think and act; it is not only the subject who speaks the discourse, but the discourse that speaks the subject.
Discursive subjectification is an active practice where individuals engage with discourses by ignoring, avoiding, or confirming them, thereby becoming 'themselves'.
Psychiatric diagnoses are examples of power relations, where a 'mad subject' is constituted precisely in relation to the authority that declares them mad.

key_concepts.definition: A coherence of meanings and representations that makes it possible to see, think, speak, understand, and act in specific ways, while simultaneously excluding other ways.
key_concepts.role: Serves as the foundational structure in poststructuralism that sets the conditions for human action and thought.

key_concepts.definition: The active processes through which people take up, ignore, avoid, or confirm discourses.
key_concepts.role: Explains how subjectification is not just a passive, hidden process, but an active engagement where individuals navigate available discourses.

key_concepts.definition: The process through which people are made into subjects via discourses and power mechanisms in all contexts they participate in.
key_concepts.role: Replaces traditional psychological concepts of an innate personality with a socially and discursively constructed identity.

key_concepts.definition: The relational force that produces subjects and categories, such as defining who is considered 'mentally ill' versus 'normal'.
key_concepts.role: Highlights that identity and personality categories are political and relational products rather than objective truths.

distinctions.label: At 'være' vs. at 'gøre' personlighed (Being vs. Doing personality)
distinctions.summary: The contrast between viewing personality as an innate, static essence (being) versus viewing it as an active, ongoing construction shaped by subjectification mechanisms (doing).
distinctions.stakes: Fundamentally shifts the psychological focus from internal traits to external power dynamics and discursive practices.

distinctions.label: Naturvidenskabelig vs. Samfundsvidenskabelig psykologi
distinctions.summary: Natural science approaches (like trait theory) aim to control, predict, and explain, whereas social science approaches (like sociocultural/poststructuralist theories) aim to comprehend, change, and develop through critique.
distinctions.stakes: Frames the epistemological goal of poststructuralism as critical and transformative rather than predictive.

likely_misunderstandings: Students might assume discourses completely and passively determine behavior, missing the active nature of 'diskursive praksisser' where subjects can ignore or refuse specific discourses.
Students might confuse poststructuralism's view of the subject with humanistic or existential views, missing Foucault's specific emphasis on power and external subjectification mechanisms.

quote_targets.target: Det er dermed ikke blot subjektet, der taler diskursen, men i høj grad også diskursen, der taler subjektet.
quote_targets.why: Captures the core poststructuralist inversion of agency and language from Staunæs & Juelskjær (2014).

quote_targets.target: “Personlighed” eller “identitet” er altså noget, man “gør” og får gjort gennem subjektiveringsmekanismer, fremfor noget, man “er”
quote_targets.why: Directly contrasts the poststructuralist view of identity with traditional personality psychology.

quote_targets.target: the mental ill person is constituted as a mad subject precisely in relation to and over against the one who declares him mad
quote_targets.why: Provides a clear, concrete example of Foucault's concept of power and subjectification in a psychiatric context.

grounding_notes: The slides heavily rely on quotes from Staunæs & Juelskjær (2014) and Davies (1990) to define discourse and discursive practices.
Foucault (1997) is used as the primary theoretical framework for understanding power, truth, and subjectification.
warnings: The slides contain specific group work questions about Foucault (1997) pages 290-299, but the answers to these questions are not provided in the slide text.
The case study on 'Inside the Manosphere' relies on an external YouTube video; the slides only provide analytical questions, not the content of the case itself.
